_meta.note: book-quiz tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: Thinking, Fast and Slow · Dopamine Master Quiz
html_002: We’re far less rational than we think
html_003: Thinking, Fast and Slow · Master Quiz
html_004: “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is,
html_005: while you are thinking about it.”
html_007: The Nobel-laureate psychologist laid bare how the mind works — its biases and key ideas — and you master them in
html_009:
.
        They start easy and get steadily harder,
html_010: and wrong ones come back until you get them right.
html_027: This is where the free preview ends
html_040: Thinking, Fast and Slow at a glance
html_043: and you’ll never forget it. It all starts with
html_047: 🧠 The mind runs on two systems
html_054: Anchoring · Availability · Representativeness · Regression to the mean
html_057: Prospect theory / Loss aversion / Endowment effect
html_059: 🎞️ The remembering self judges your life
html_062: Table of contents at a glance
html_064: 3 cases from the book worth pondering
html_066: Focused review of what you missed
html_068: lets you watch a talk by Daniel Kahneman himself.
html_074: A repeat master quiz for learning a book’s core with dopamine
html_075: This app is secondary study content that reworks the book’s core concepts for learning purposes. For the full reading experience, meet the original book itself.
html_076: Thinking, Fast and Slow · book info

js_004: Who wrote Thinking, Fast and Slow?
js_009: Daniel Kahneman is a psychologist and the father of behavioral economics, who won the Nobel Prize in Economics in 2002.
js_011: Which prize did Kahneman receive in 2002?
js_013: The Nobel Prize in Medicine, for psychology
js_015: The Fields Medal, math’s top honor
js_016: It’s rare for a psychologist to win the Nobel Prize in Economics — the honor came for exposing how irrationally we judge.
js_018: What are the two axes this book splits human thinking into?
js_023: Kahneman describes the mind as two characters: System 1 (fast thinking) and System 2 (slow thinking).
js_025: Which thinking runs fast and automatically, with almost no effort?
js_029: System 1 reacts instantly and automatically — the way 2 plus 2 pops up as 4 before you even try.
js_031: Which thinking handles complex calculations that take focus and effort?
js_032: System 1, which pops up on its own
js_035: System 2 takes on the jobs that demand attention and effort, like 17 × 24. Its flaw? It’s a bit lazy.
js_037: Which best matches the trait Kahneman pins on System 2?
js_038: A slacker that just rubber-stamps System 1
js_040: A flawless calculator that never slips up
js_041: A fickle mind swayed by its emotions
js_042: System 2 hates hard work, so it usually just waves System 1’s answer through — and bias slips in through that gap.
js_043: What do we call System 1’s habit of jumping to conclusions on only what it sees?
js_044: What you see is all there is; what you don’t see, you ignore
js_045: You only see as much as you know
js_047: Seeing once beats hearing a hundred times
js_048: WYSIATI — What You See Is All There Is. System 1 ignores what’s missing and builds a story from only what’s there.
js_050: What’s the phenomenon where we trust familiar, easy-to-read information more?
js_051: Trusting things more the stranger they are
js_052: Believing the harder it is, the truer it is
js_053: Thinking more information means more accuracy
js_054: Familiar, crisp sentences feel truer thanks to cognitive ease. But familiarity isn’t truth.
js_056: What’s it called when the first number you see drags your later judgments toward it?
js_059: The bandwagon effect of following the crowd
js_060: The recency effect, pulled by later info
js_061: Anchoring. Even an absurd number, seen first, keeps our estimates hovering right around it.
js_063: Which shortcut estimates probability from whatever examples come to mind easily?
js_066: The heuristic that clings to the first number
js_068: The availability heuristic. After vivid news of an accident, we feel the risk is far bigger than it is.
js_074: Talks at Google · a talk by Daniel Kahneman
js_075: Kahneman studied the errors in human judgment with his collaborator Amos Tversky. Tversky passed away first, so the Nobel went to Kahneman alone.
js_076: A psychologist won an economics prize because his experiments shook economics’ core assumption that people are always rational.
js_077: System 1 and 2 aren’t two real regions of the brain — they’re metaphorical characters that aid understanding. This one frame threads the whole book together.
js_078: Flinching at a scary photo, or an easy sum popping into your head — that’s all System 1. You can’t switch it off even if you want to.
js_079: System 2 has a small capacity. That’s why your steps stall when you try hard multiplication while walking.
js_080: System 2’s laziness is the heart of the problem. Even when it should double-check, it just trusts System 1’s gut.
js_081: WYSIATI is the source of both confidence and bias. The less information there is, the smoother the story — and the more sure we feel.
js_082: Clear type, repeated phrases, rhyming lines — all feel truer. That’s why ads repeat themselves.
js_083: In one experiment, judges rolled dice and then set a sentence — and the verdict drifted toward whatever number came up. That’s how strong anchoring is.
js_084: Availability is swayed by recency and vividness. When divorce is all over the news, we guess the divorce rate is higher than it is.
js_098: The mind has a fast, automatic System 1 and a slow, effortful System 2. That’s the book’s big premise.
js_099: What you see is all there is (WYSIATI)
js_100: System 1 ignores missing information and spins a smooth story from what’s there, making us overconfident.
js_102: The number you see first pulls your later estimates toward it. Even an unrelated number moves your judgment — a powerful bias.
js_104: If something comes to mind easily or looks typical, we misjudge its probability. Convenient shortcuts breed bias.
js_106: Extremes are followed by the ordinary. Miss this and you’ll invent causes that aren’t there — like the ‘effects’ of praise or punishment.
js_108: For the same amount, the pain of losing outweighs the joy of gaining. Prospect theory captures this asymmetry in a graph.
js_110: The same fact is judged differently depending on how it’s framed — and on the moment it becomes yours.
js_112: The self that lives the moment and the self that remembers the story are different. The peak–end rule rules memory.
js_114: The mind has two characters: fast, automatic System 1, and slow, effortful System 2.
js_115: System 1 endlessly generates impressions and intuitions, and System 2 usually just signs off on them.
js_116: System 2 has little capacity and is lazy, so self-control and focus run dry fast.
js_117: WYSIATI: System 1 builds a story from only what’s available, breeding overconfidence.
js_118: Cognitive ease, association and priming sketch the outline of our judgments without our noticing.
js_121: Anchoring: the number you see first pulls your later estimates — even an unrelated one.
js_122: Availability: estimating probability from easy-to-recall cases, we over- or under-rate risk.
js_123: Representativeness: if it fits a typical image, we ignore base rates and jump to conclusions (the Linda problem).
js_124: Regression to the mean and the law of small numbers: the trap of mistaking chance for cause.
js_125: The takeaway: we judge by plausible stories, not by probability.
js_128: The narrative fallacy: we tidy the past into a smooth story and believe we understand the world better than we do.
js_129: The illusion of validity: we make confident predictions even on flimsy grounds.
js_130: Simple formulas often beat expert intuition. Consistency is the strength.
js_131: When intuition can be trusted: a regular environment plus enough feedback to learn from.
js_132: Planning fallacy & optimism bias: we habitually overestimate schedules and odds of success.
js_135: Bernoulli’s error: expected-utility theory fails to explain the choices people actually make.
js_136: Prospect theory: we judge from a reference point, and feel losses more than gains (loss aversion).
js_137: The endowment effect: the moment something is yours its value jumps, and asking price parts from buying price.
js_138: The fourfold pattern: facing a sure loss we gamble, and at small probabilities we buy lottery tickets and insurance.
js_139: Framing: the same fact flips your choice depending on whether it’s framed as a gain or a loss.
js_141: The experiencing self lives the moment; the remembering self writes the story afterward.
js_142: The peak–end rule: the memory of pain is set by its most intense moment and how it ended.
js_143: Duration neglect: how long it lasted barely registers in memory.
js_144: The focusing illusion: nothing is as important as it seems while you’re thinking about it.
js_145: The takeaway: you can’t erase bias entirely, but noticing it moves you toward better judgment.
js_147: A bat and a ball cost $1.10 together, and the bat costs $1 more than the ball — so how much is the ball? Most people blurt out 10 cents, but the answer is 5 cents. System 2 waved through System 1’s answer without checking.
js_148: This simple problem trips up even smart people — and shows our lazy System 2 more clearly than anything.
js_150: Linda studied philosophy and cared deeply about social justice — is she just a bank teller, or a feminist bank teller? Most pick the latter, but the more conditions you add the lower the probability, so it’s logically the wrong answer.
js_151: It’s a case that catches, in a clean experiment, the moment a plausible story beats the laws of probability.
js_153: Patients whose procedure was stretched a little longer but ended on milder pain remembered it as less painful than those who finished quickly but on a sharp note. The peak and the end ruled their memory, not the total amount of pain.
js_154: It’s the landmark experiment proving that the experiencing self and the remembering self diverge — and demonstrating the peak–end rule.
js_155: How far can I trust my intuition? Is this a regular field, and have I had enough feedback to learn from?
js_156: Try separating where intuition works (chess, the ER) from where it doesn’t (stock picking, political forecasts).
js_157: Am I clinging to something that’s already over, just because of loss aversion?
js_158: Think of sunk cost and the endowment effect, and ask: if I were starting fresh today, would I choose the same thing?
js_159: Am I living for my experiencing self, or for my remembering self?
js_160: Look back: have you ever missed the moment while trying to capture the photo? And ask what truly makes you happy.
js_164: You’ve got almost all the core logic
js_166: You’ve learned the skeleton — one more round!
js_168: The more you repeat, the higher your score

_app.title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
_app.desc: Master the mind’s quirks and biases from the Nobel-laureate psychologist, one dopamine quiz at a time
